coredata.dc:title: Experiment-based conceptualization of an automated disassembly process chain for the recycling of lithium-ion battery modules
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:issueName: 10th CIRP Conference on Assembly Technology and Systems (CIRP CATS 2024)- 10th CIRP Conference on Assembly Technology and Systems (CIRP CATS 2024)
coredata.dc:creator: Hölter, Timo
Dierksen, Fynn Hendrik
Dröder, Klaus
coredata.dc:description: As the volume of end-of-life lithium-ion batteries (LIB) used in electric vehicles increases, so does the demand for recycling to recover valuable active materials as secondary resources, reducing the need for primary raw materials and minimizing supply dependencies. In addition to pyro- and hydrometallurgy recycling processes, an increasingly examined approach is the direct recycling route, which involves disassembling battery systems into modules, modules to cells, and ultimately cells to electrodes. To minimize passive materials such as various metals from the housing, joints, arresters, plastics, foams, and adhesives in the subsequent crushed mass, the introduction of module disassembly processes into the existing LIB recycling process chain is promising for the resulting increase in recyclate purity. This study presents the conceptualization of a LIB module disassembly process chain which aims to elevate the depth of disassembly of LIB during early recycling stages. A reverse engineering approach is used to recover individual cells from two battery modules of one specific automotive manufacturer by removing their casing through milling and disconnecting the cells from the arresters. The experimental results are analyzed for influences on the disassembly possibilities for individual components as well as the interactions within the system, enabling the elaboration of a priority graph and a disassembly plan. In order to allow for scalable recycling process chains suitable for very high quantities, a concept for a fully automated and variant-compatible robot application based on the experiment is derived. Furthermore, the depth of disassembly is compared with the corresponding reduction of passive materials in the crushed mass. The findings indicate that several module components can be disassembled, resulting in cell recovery and a reduction of metal and plastic in the crushed mass by about 17 %, highlighting the importance of robotic disassembly in enhancing material separation efficiency during LIB recycling.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-Ion Batteries
Recycling
Automated Disassembly
Robot-based Automation
Reverse Engineering

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was conducted within the research project HVBatCycle (FKZ: 16BZF342D), funded by the Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action of Germany.
This research was conducted within the research project \u201CHVBatCycle\u201D (FKZ: 16BZF342D), funded by the Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action of Germany.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.com Procedia CIRP 127 (2024) 249â€“254 2212-8271 Â© 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0) Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 10th CIRP Conference on Assembly Technology and Systems 10.1016/j.procir.2024.07.043 Keywords: Lithium-Ion Batteries; Recycling; Automated Disassembly; Robot-based Automation; Reverse Engineering 1. Introduction Electric vehicles (EV) with lithium-ion batteries (LIB) are increasingly relevant as part of the automotive industry's decarbonization strategy. In the long term, the increasing number of EV will lead to an increasing quantity of end-of-life vehicles and spent LIB for disposal. The International Energy Agency expects the number of spent LIB to increase from 100 GWh in 2030 to 400 GWh in 2035 and 1,300 GWh in 2040 [1]. Improper disposal of these LIB poses health, fire and environmental risks and is a major challenge [1]. For these reasons, battery recycling is imperative and in addition of economic interest due to the valuable materials contained in LIB. The estimated salvage value of recycled LIB ranges from 15 $/kWh to 42 $/kWh depending on the cathode chemistry [2]. Besides copper, the most valuable materials are nickel, cobalt and lithium whose primary raw material production is concentrated in just a few countries. According to the U.S. Geological Survey, in 2022, 48 % of global nickel production came from Indonesia, 68 % of global cobalt production from Congo, and 47 % of global lithium production from Australia [3]. Particularly from a European perspective, there is a high dependency on imports of these materials, making recycling essential for the development of a sustainable and self- Experiment-based conceptualization of an automated disassembly process chain for the recycling of lithium-ion battery modules Timo HÃ¶ltera,b,*, Fynn Hendrik Dierksena, Klaus DrÃ¶dera,b,c aTU Braunschweig, Institute of Machine Tools and Production Technology, Langer Kamp 19b, 38106 Braunschweig, Germany bTU Braunschweig, Battery LabFactory Braunschweig, Langer Kamp 8, 38106 Braunschweig, Germany cFraunhofer Institute for Surface Engineering and Thin Films IST, Riedenkamp 2, 38108 Braunschweig, Germany * Corresponding author. Tel.: +49 531-391-7680; E-mail address: timo.hoelter@tu-braunschweig.de Abstract As the volume of end-of-life lithium-ion batteries (LIB) used in electric vehicles increases, so does the demand for recycling to recover valuable active materials as secondary resources, reducing the need for primary raw materials and minimizing supply dependencies. In addition to pyro- and hydrometallurgy recycling processes, an increasingly examined approach is the direct recycling route, which involves disassembling battery systems into modules, modules to cells, and ultimately cells to electrodes. To minimize passive materials such as various metals from the housing, joints, arresters, plastics, foams, and adhesives in the subsequent crushed mass, the introduction of module disassembly processes into the existing LIB recycling process chain is promising for the resulting increase in recyclate purity. This study presents the conceptualization of a LIB module disassembly process chain which aims to elevate the depth of disassembly of LIB during early recycling stages. A reverse engineering approach is used to recover individual cells from two battery modules of one specific automotive manufacturer by removing their casing through milling and disconnecting the cells from the arresters. The experimental results are analyzed for influences on the disassembly possibilities for individual components as well as the interactions within the system, enabling the elaboration of a priority graph and a disassembly plan. In order to allow for scalable recycling process chains suitable for very high quantities, a concept for a fully automated and variant-compatible robot application based on the experiment is derived. Furthermore, the depth of disassembly is compared with the corresponding reduction of passive materials in the crushed mass. The findings indicate that several module components can be disassembled, resulting in cell recovery and a reduction of metal and plastic in the crushed mass by about 17 %, highlighting the importance of robotic disassembly in enhancing material separation efficiency during LIB recycling. 10th CIRP Conference on Assembly Technology and Systems (CIRP CATS 2024) Â© 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0) Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 10th CIRP Conference on Assembly Technology and Systems 250 Timo HÃ¶lter et al. / Procedia CIRP 127 (2024) 249â€“254 sufficient circular economy. This interest is reaffirmed by the European Parliament's adoption of the updated battery directive in June 2023 [4] which requires 50 % of lithium (Li) and 90 % each of cobalt (Co), copper (Cu), lead (Pb) and nickel (Ni) to be recovered from spent batteries by 2027. By 2031, this share must increase to 80 % Li and 95 % each for Co, Cu, Pb and Ni. Additionally, the directive sets minimum requirements for the content of recycled materials in new batteries, as shown in Fig. 1. Fig. 1. Excerpt from the European Parliament's updated battery directive [4]. Battery recycling is a diverse process, combining between two and 14 different processes into different recycling routes [5], which can be broadly divided into two main areas: pyro- metallurgy and hydrometallurgy [6]. The first step in battery recycling is the pre-treatment which consists of discharging the module and producing the black mass, in particular by crushing the module and initial density and magnetic separations [6,7]. In the pyrometallurgical process, the black mass is thermally treated with temperatures up to 1450 Â°C to produce a nickel-cobalt-copper alloy and a lithium-rich slag [6,8]. Subsequent hydrometallurgical treatment is required to further increase the purity of the materials. This involves leaching out the remaining mass and separating the materials according to their chemical properties [6,7]. However, the hydrometallurgical process can also be used directly, without an upstream pyrometallurgical step, and is known as the LithoRec process [9]. Another alternative recycling method is direct recycling, which, unlike pyro- and hydro-metallurgy, does not require crushing. In contrast, the discharged modules are disassembled into cells, and subsequently into electrodes. One advantage of this method is the enabled possibility of delaminating electrodes with solvents, thereby reducing the risk of cross- contamination with other materials, which is prevalent in processes based on crushing [5,6,10]. In addition, subsequent thermal or leaching regeneration processes only have to be carried out with smaller quantities [11]. Module disassembly is therefore essential for direct recycling, but increasing the depth of the disassembly is also an advantageous upstream process step for pyro- and hydro- metallurgy as it reduces the proportion of passive materials in the crushed mass. The goal is to introduce module disassembly as an additional process step in any recycling route or to demonstrate its advantages especially in large-scale applications. The present study applies reverse engineering through manual disassembly of two modules to subsequently derive a disassembly strategy in the form of a priority graph. Furthermore, conceptual disassembly plans for automation are being developed. 2. Reverse Engineering approach The primary objective of the focused module disassembly process is to recover the individual cells from the aluminum housing without damaging the pouch foil or the sealing seams. The process steps required for this are described in more detail using the example of two widely used module variants from a specific automotive manufacturer, to specifically focus its disassembly strategy. Both modules have 24 pouch cells and approximately the same geometric external dimensions, nominal capacity and nominal energy. However, the interior design or configurations of the modules, and thus the disassembly procedure, differs in some aspects. 2.1. Manual disassembly The following describes the disassembly procedures for the two modules and their differences. For safety reasons, the modules have been short-circuited with a cable for manual disassembly. This will not be necessary for future large-scale applications as the discharging will be the recycling step directly upstream. For both modules, the first steps are milling and removing the aluminum (Al) lid and a cover beneath. The top cover for Module 1 (M1) is made of a composite material whereas for Module 2 (M2) it is made of plastic. The next step is to mill and remove the left and right Al sides, which exposes a layer of foam on each side. In M1, an additional layer of composite material must be removed separately. The front and rear cast aluminum sides are then milled open. Beneath these, except for the front of M1, there is an additional plastic cover, which can also be removed manually. Now the battery diagnostic components are exposed for both modules, which consists of a connector plate at the front being connected to a rear plate by a flat cable. The design and therefore the disassembly of the battery diagnostic components and arresters differs greatly between the modules. Fig. 2. Schematic illustrations of the arrester plates of Module 1 (left) and Module 2 (right) which represents the biggest difference Timo HÃ¶lter et al. / Procedia CIRP 127 (2024) 249â€“254 251 As shown in Fig. 2., the diagnostic plates in M1 are just attached by plastic rivets, which can be pried out by force. In contrast, the diagnostic components of M2 are soldered to the arrester plates and fastened with two screws each. The diagnostic plate contacts are milled out and the screws are unscrewed. Next, the individual cells or rather their tabs are disconnected from the arresters and thus from the arrester plates. The design and disassembly process also differs in this step, as shown in Fig. 2. In M1, the tabs are passed through the arrester plate to the front in groups of three and contacted there. The tabs are therefore easily accessible and can be disconnected with eight milling cuts. In contrast, the tabs in M2 are contacted individually. In addition, the arrester plate is attached to the bottom Al plate by four screws, whereat these screws are again replaced by plastic rivets on M1. To avoid milling the 24 contacts separately and unscrewing the four screws, a sawing operation is used. After cutting the tabs and removing the arrestor plates, the individual cells stand on the bottom Al plate and are held in place by an adhesive conductive silicone. The cells are glued together and each six cells are separated by a layer of foam. For separating the cells, the solvent isopropyl alcohol is applied onto the outermost cell until it is entirely separated from the neighboring cell, whereby the cell also partly detaches from the adhesive conductive silicone of the bottom Al plate. To detach the cell completely, it is necessary to move the cell back and forth several times and exert a pulling force. This is done for all 24 cells and in the end, only the bottom Al plate remains. 2.2. Priority graph The sequence of the individual process steps for module disassembly, which were presented in chapter 2.1, is partially interchangeable. Therefore, a priority graph for the maximum disassembly depth is created which includes both modules and from which various disassembly plans can be derived. It is illustrated in Fig. 3. According to the priority graph, the milling of the housing and the subsequent handling can be done in parallel with respect to the ranking. To disassemble the diagnostic components, the lid and front/back sides must be removed in advance. As mentioned, the process of disassembling the diagnostic components varies significantly between the module variants. In M2, the diagnostic plate contacts must first be disconnected followed by removing the screws. This sequence is important because otherwise there is a risk of the diagnostic components falling onto the milling cutter, or rather because it is much easier to handle the components while unscrewing than while milling. There are no metal contacts or screws at M1 diagnostic components, instead they are held in place by plastic rivets, eliminating the need to use the "unscrewing" disassembly process. The same applies to disconnecting the cells. In M2, the tabs are contacted individually and the arrester plate is screwed from below, so a saw cut proved to be the most suitable disconnecting process. In the case of M1, the tabs can be easily disconnected by milling, so the "sawing" disassembly process can be omitted here. After handling the arrester plate, the 24 cells can be recovered individually by dissolving the adhesive. The subsequent recovery and handling of the cells are summarized in the next step. The removal of the remaining bottom Al plate represents the last process. Fig. 3. Priority graph derived from the manual disassembly of Module 1 (orange) and Module 2 (green) 252 Timo HÃ¶lter et al. / Procedia CIRP 127 (2024) 249â€“254 2.3. Reduction of passive materials Module disassembly is an indispensable process step for direct recycling. But also for pyro- and hydrometallurgy, the share of passive materials in the crushed mass can be drastically reduced by module disassembly. The exact material composition of the modules in terms of mass, with M1 weighing 30.2 kg and M2 weighing 30.4 kg, is described in more detail. For this purpose, the individual components were weighed after disassembly, which is why a loss of mass due to machining must be taken into account. Fig. 4 visualizes the material composition by mass. Both modules are very similar in composition. About 83 % of the mass can be attributed to the recovered cells. The remaining 17 % must be disassembled to reach the maximum disassembly depth. Of these 17 %, about 12 % are aluminum sheet or cast housing components. In M2, a further 2.4 % can be attributed to plastics and foams; in M1, the value is also 2.4 % when composites are included. A further 1.2 % is made up of the tin-coated copper of the arresters. Steel is merely used for nuts and bolts with only 0.05 % for M1 and approximately 0.16 % for M2. This difference is a result of M1 implementing plastic rivets instead of screws inside, and the steel components are only required for the short circuit cable attachment which, as described, would not be present in later large-scale applications. Another difference is the diagnostic components, which are significantly larger in size in M2. The remainder is grouped under 'other' and is attributed to metal chips, sawdust and other non-measurable components due to the dissolving of the adhesives. Fig. 4. Material composition by mass of Module 1 (top) and Module 2 (bottom) 3. Disassembly plans for automation The automation of the module disassembly process is an essential research focus. In this case, it is derived conceptually on the basis of a planned robotic cell. In addition, a computed tomography (CT) scan will be performed in preparation for disassembly. This allows the interior of the module to be analyzed and the position and orientation of the components and joints to be determined. If the misalignments cannot be handled by the robot, the module is ejected. However, the exact definition of these limitations will not be described in detail here. As speed or cycle time plays a secondary role in the first stage, the use of a single robot with a robotic tool changing system and a rotary table is planned. The following disassembly plans are created from the perspective of the tool changes required by the robot. First of all, a variant-compatible clamping device for the modules on the rotary table must be provided. To ensure all edges are accessible for the subsequent robot-guided milling operations, vacuum clamping is planned in order to guarantee ideally full-faced suction on the bottom Al plate surface. The absorption of transverse and longitudinal forces during milling is the most important requirement for the respective vacuum technology. To prevent the four aluminum sides from falling or tilting uncontrollably, which could destroy the milling cutter in the worst case, they must be held down mechanically. The further requirements for an automated disassembly, in particular those on the robot side, are now described by module. 3.1. Disassembly plan for Module 1 For the disassembly of M1, a handling tool is required in addition to the milling cutter. The handling tool should be able to perform all relevant handling operations, which is made possible by the use of flexible and adjustable vacuum technology. For this purpose, the handling tool is designed as a multi-effector, shaped like a cube, on the sides of which various vacuum and gripper technologies can be mounted. The requirements for the handling tool are described in more detail using the following disassembly plan. The first handling step is to place the module on the rotary table. Due to the flat surface of the Al lid, an appropriate vacuum gripper can move the approximately 30 kg. After milling, the individual housing components can be handled with the same vacuum gripper. As the handling surfaces of the sides are thinner than the lid, the vacuum gripper can only have the dimensions of the front and back sides or many smaller vacuum grippers can be used in a modular way to realize the handling. The respective covers can also be handled with this vacuum gripper. A handling of the diagnostic components must be conducted in the same step. As these components are too small and bulky for a flat vacuum gripper, a mechanical gripper finger is used to pick up, break out and remove the diagnostic plates one at a time. After milling of the tabs, the arrester plates must be removed. Since these are also relatively bulky and, more importantly, are not airtight due to the tab bypasses, either many small vacuum grippers have to be used locally or a mechanical gripper finger as described above has to be used again. Timo HÃ¶lter et al. / Procedia CIRP 127 (2024) 249â€“254 253 The recovery process and handling of the cells poses special requirements. A possible approach is the implementation of large-area suction by vacuum technology to tilt the outer cell by a constant pulling force from the robot. This creates a small gap between two cells, into which a plate is inserted, separating the cells and tilting the outer cell. If the adhesive is too strong for direct mechanical separation, this can be supplemented with automatic isopropyl alcohol wetting to prevent damaging the pouch foil. The cells are then pulled out from the inside by suction. The final step is the handling of the bottom Al plate, which is also possible with a vacuum gripper. In summary, three different handling grippers are required for the multi- effector. These are visualized in Fig. 5 by different illustrations. A total of four tool changes per cycle are necessary. Fig. 5. Disassembly plan for Module 1, marked illustrations from [12] 3.2. Disassembly plan for Module 2 The disassembly for M2 follows a similar structure. As described in the priority graph, the disassembly of M2 implements sawing and unscrewing operations in addition to milling. Therefore, both a screwdriving system and a stationary saw for the robot-guided sawing operations are necessary. The first difference is, that the side walls are not milled at the beginning, as they are needed as relevant clamping surfaces for the later sawing operation. Another difference is the disassembly of the diagnostic components, which requires both milling and unscrewing, and therefore two tool changes. The subsequent robot-guided sawing process poses particular requirements on the gripper due to the transverse and longitudinal forces involved. Vacuum grippers are designed for this purpose only to a limited extent [12], so an alternative handling system is initially proposed, in which the module is mechanically clamped onto the entire surfaces of the side walls. Once the sawing process is complete, there is no need to handle the arrester plate as it is removed via a chute directly at the saw. After the sawing operation is complete and the module is clamped back onto the rotary table, the side walls are milled and removed. The rest of the disassembly process, especially the recovery of the cells, remains the same as in M1. Four different handling grippers are required for the multi-effector mentioned earlier, as visualized in Fig. 6. A total of seven tool changes are required per cycle. Fig. 6. Disassembly plan for Module 2, marked illustrations from [12] 3.3. Alternative disassembly plans The disassembly plans can be very different, depending on the circumstances and the requirements, for example the number of robots, the disassembly depth or the further recycling steps such as crushing or cell disassembly. The disassembly plans presented focus on a high disassembly depth by removing many of the individual components separately and ultimately recovering the cells using just one robot. In the following, additional approaches to other circumstances and requirements are presented. Firstly, incorporating a second robot would be highly beneficial especially for large-scale applications. This would enable simultaneous handling and milling, simplify the vacuum clamping device on the rotary table and greatly reduce the number of tool changes and therefore the cycle time. An additional approach is to focus solely on cell recovery, rather than on all of the individual housing and arrester components. The idea is to completely saw off the front and back sides as a first step, including the cast aluminum front side, the tinned copper arresters, the plastic arrester plate and the diagnostic components. Subsequent milling and handling of the lid and side walls would expose the cells much faster. A disadvantage of this approach is that the sawn-off front and back contain the wide range of materials described above and may therefore need to be further disassembled in secondary processes to achieve maximum separation. If the next recycling step is not cell recovery itself, but the crushing of the cells, a different approach is possible. The concept is to leave the cells on the bottom plate without recovering them one by one. This could be advantageous, as 254 Timo HÃ¶lter et al. / Procedia CIRP 127 (2024) 249â€“254 cell recovery and handling are considered the most critical process steps due to the presence of the adhesive conductive silicone on the bottom plate and the health and safety risks associated with a possible rupture of the pouch foil. However, this approach would result in approximately 4 % aluminum sheet and the conductive silicone, which is part of the components categorized as "other". The maximum disassembly depth would therefore not be achieved, resulting in contamination with passive materials and reduced recyclate purity. 4. Conclusion and outlook The goal was to use reverse engineering to develop a disassembly strategy for two different LIB modules with conceptual disassembly plans for automation. By manually disassembling two different battery modules, their structure and material composition could be analyzed and evaluated with regard to a variant-compatible disassembling strategy. The resulting priority graph and disassembly plans methodically and conceptually show fully automated module disassembly approaches with different depths of disassembly depending on the focus. In perspective, it is evident that using a number of robots to parallelize the various processes, in particular milling and handling, reduces the cycle time. Ultimately, it is shown that passive materials, which account for 17 % of the initial module mass, can be removed by module disassembly, thus facilitating subsequent recycling steps by increasing the recyclate purity. This highlights the importance of module disassembly as an additional pre-treatment process step. Possible future research developments include the validation of the described vacuum gripper concepts, with a specific focus on cell recovery. In the next stage of this development, computer-aided design and subsequent manufacturing should be targeted, followed by robot disassembly experiments, testing both milling and handling. These two points are expected to be the most challenging factors for the disassembly. When milling, it is crucial to match the exact geometry to avoid damaging the underlying cell. To ensure tolerance, position and orientation compensation, it is necessary to use appropriate sensor technology, such as an image-processing camera system in combination with the envisaged CT scan. This is also useful for handling operations, as the position and orientation of the disassembled components can vary. The project partners are currently developing modularly controllable vacuum grippers that are adapted to the requirements and can be controlled via real-time computing fieldbus systems. During the build-up process, it is necessary to develop an improved safety concept that includes sensor technology to detect gas development caused by damaged cells. Another important aspect is the design for recycling, in order to standardize future disassembly. It has become clear, that even similar modules can necessitate very different disassembly process routes. In order to ensure efficient disassembly processes across manufacturers, it is essential to consider future recycling processes at the end-of-life as part of the product development process. In this context, both the economic and ecological impacts need to be investigated further. Acknowledgement This research was conducted within the research project â€œHVBatCycleâ€� (FKZ: 16BZF342D), funded by the Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action of Germany. Robot Grippers. 2006. https://doi.org/10.1002/9783527610280 obot disassembly experiments, testing both milling and handling. These two points are expected to be the most challenging factors for the disassembly. When milling, it is crucial to match the exact geometry to avoid damaging the underlying cell. To ensure tolerance, position and orientation compensation, it is necessary to use appropriate sensor technology, such as an image-processing camera system in combination with the envisaged CT scan. This is also useful for handling operations, as the position and orientation of the disassembled components can vary. The project partners are currently developing modularly controllable vacuum grippers that are adapted to the requirements and can be controlled via real-time computing fieldbus systems. During the build-up process, it is necessary to develop an improved safety concept that includes sensor technology to detect gas development caused by damaged cells. Another important aspect is the design for recycling, in order to standardize future disassembly. It has become clear, that even similar modules can necessitate very different disassembly process routes. In order to ensure efficient disassembly processes across manufacturers, it is essential to consider future recycling processes at the end-of-life as part of the product development process. In this context, both the economic and ecological impacts need to be investigated further. head.ce:title: Experiment-based conceptualization of an automated disassembly process chain for the recycling of lithium-ion battery modules

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: TU Braunschweig, Institute of Machine Tools and Production Technology, Langer Kamp 19b, 38106 Braunschweig, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Machine Tools and Production Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aTU Braunschweig, Institute of Machine Tools and Production Technology, Langer Kamp 19b, 38106 Braunschweig, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: TU Braunschweig, Battery LabFactory Braunschweig, Langer Kamp 8, 38106 Braunschweig, Germany
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bTU Braunschweig, Battery LabFactory Braunschweig, Langer Kamp 8, 38106 Braunschweig, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fraunhofer Institute for Surface Engineering and Thin Films IST, Riedenkamp 2, 38108 Braunschweig, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fraunhofer Institute for Surface Engineering and Thin Films IST

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cFraunhofer Institute for Surface Engineering and Thin Films IST, Riedenkamp 2, 38108 Braunschweig, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As the volume of end-of-life lithium-ion batteries (LIB) used in electric vehicles increases, so does the demand for recycling to recover valuable active materials as secondary resources, reducing the need for primary raw materials and minimizing supply dependencies. In addition to pyro- and hydrometallurgy recycling processes, an increasingly examined approach is the direct recycling route, which involves disassembling battery systems into modules, modules to cells, and ultimately cells to electrodes. To minimize passive materials such as various metals from the housing, joints, arresters, plastics, foams, and adhesives in the subsequent crushed mass, the introduction of module disassembly processes into the existing LIB recycling process chain is promising for the resulting increase in recyclate purity. This study presents the conceptualization of a LIB module disassembly process chain which aims to elevate the depth of disassembly of LIB during early recycling stages. A reverse engineering approach is used to recover individual cells from two battery modules of one specific automotive manufacturer by removing their casing through milling and disconnecting the cells from the arresters. The experimental results are analyzed for influences on the disassembly possibilities for individual components as well as the interactions within the system, enabling the elaboration of a priority graph and a disassembly plan. In order to allow for scalable recycling process chains suitable for very high quantities, a concept for a fully automated and variant-compatible robot application based on the experiment is derived. Furthermore, the depth of disassembly is compared with the corresponding reduction of passive materials in the crushed mass. The findings indicate that several module components can be disassembled, resulting in cell recovery and a reduction of metal and plastic in the crushed mass by about 17 %, highlighting the importance of robotic disassembly in enhancing material separation efficiency during LIB recycling.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: International Energy Agency. The Role of Critical Minerals in Clean Energy Transitions (full report); 2021. Available online (accessed on 07 August 2023): https://www.iea.org/reports/the-role-of-critical-minerals-in-clean-energy-transitions
